sections.title: Numbering Systems
sections.content:
## Decimal, Binary, and Hexadecimal

Assembly language programmers must be fluent in three numbering systems: **decimal** (base-10), **binary** (base-2), and **hexadecimal** (base-16).

### Why These Systems Matter

- **Decimal**: The number system humans use naturally
- **Binary**: How computers represent all dataвҖ”zeros and ones
- **Hexadecimal**: A human-friendly way to represent binary data

### Conversions

| Decimal | Binary | Hexadecimal |
|---------|--------|-------------|
| 0 | 0000 | 0 |
| 1 | 0001 | 1 |
| 9 | 1001 | 9 |
| 10 | 1010 | A |
| 15 | 1111 | F |

### Binary to Hexadecimal

Group binary digits in sets of four, starting from the right:

```
Binary:  11010110 00111101
Group:  1101 0110 0011 1101
Hex:     D    6    3    D
```

### Hexadecimal to Decimal

Each hex digit represents 4 binary bits. The rightmost digit is worth 16вҒ°, the next 16В№, and so on:

```
3D7h = (3 Г— 16ВІ) + (13 Г— 16В№) + (7 Г— 16вҒ°)
     = (3 Г— 256) + (13 Г— 16) + (7 Г— 1)
     = 768 + 208 + 7
     = 983
```

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** The `h` suffix denotes hexadecimal in assembly (e.g., `FFh`).

sections.title: Data Representation Fundamentals
sections.content:
## How Computers Store Data

### Bits and Bytes

- **Bit**: A single binary digit (0 or 1)
- **Byte**: 8 bits. The basic unit of memory addressing.
- **Word**: 16 bits (2 bytes)
- **Doubleword (DWORD)**: 32 bits (4 bytes)
- **Quadword (QWORD)**: 64 bits (8 bytes)

### Signed vs Unsigned Numbers

#### Unsigned Numbers

Unsigned numbers can represent 0 and positive integers only.

| Size | Range |
|------|-------|
| Byte (8-bit) | 0 to 255 |
| Word (16-bit) | 0 to 65,535 |

#### Signed Numbers (Two's Complement)

Signed numbers use the most significant bit (MSB) as a sign bit.

- **0 in MSB**: Positive number
- **1 in MSB**: Negative number

| Size | Range |
|------|-------|
| Byte (8-bit) | -128 to +127 |
| Word (16-bit) | -32,768 to +32,767 |

### Two's Complement Representation

To find the negative of a number in two's complement:

1. Invert all bits
2. Add 1

```asm
; Example: Representing -42 in 8-bit two's complement
; 42 in binary = 00101010

; Step 1: Invert all bits
; 00101010 вҶ’ 11010101

; Step 2: Add 1
;     11010101
; +        1
; = 11010110

; So -42 = 11010110 in two's complement
```

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** The two's complement system allows the same addition circuitry to handle both positive and negative numbers without special cases.

sections.title: 8086 CPU Architecture
sections.content:
## The 8086 Microprocessor

The Intel 8086 is a 16-bit microprocessor introduced in 1978. It was the first member of the x86 family, which still powers modern computers.

### Key Characteristics

- **16-bit internal registers**: Can hold 16-bit values
- **20-bit address bus**: Can address up to 1 MB of memory (2ВІвҒ° = 1,048,576 bytes)
- **8-bit external data bus** (8088 variant) or 16-bit (8086)
- **Clock speeds**: 5 MHz, 8 MHz, or 10 MHz

### CPU Components

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ           8086 CPU                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ      General Registersers    в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  AX  BX  CX  DX  SP  BP  SI DI в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ      Segment Registers       в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ    CS   DS   SS   ES         в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ   IP      в”Ӯ в”Ӯ    Flags      в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ(Instructionв”Ӯ в”Ӯ   Register   в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Pointer) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ               в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Register Classification

#### General Purpose Registers (16-bit)

| Register | Name | Special Purpose |
|----------|------|------------------|
| AX | Accumulator | Primary for arithmetic operations |
| BX | Base | Base pointer for memory addressing |
| CX | Counter | Loop counts, shifts, rotations |
| DX | Data | I/O operations, multiplication |
| SP | Stack Pointer | Points to top of stack |
| BP | Base Pointer | Stack frame reference |
| SI | Source Index | String/source pointer |
| DI | Destination Index | String/destination pointer |

#### Segment Registers

| Register | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| CS | Code Segment - current code segment |
| DS | Data Segment - default data segment |
| SS | Stack Segment - stack segment |
| ES | Extra Segment - string destination |

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** While called "general purpose," each register has historical specializations that still influence optimal usage.

sections.title: Assembly Language Basics
sections.content:
## Assembly Language Syntax

### Structure of an Assembly Statement

An assembly instruction has up to four fields:

```asm
[label:]   mnemonic   [operands]   [;comment]
```

#### Examples:

```asm
; No label, single operand
INC CX

; Label with operand
start:  MOV AX, 0

; Two operands
ADD AX, BX

; Full format with label and comment
loop_start:  DEC CX    ; Decrement counter
```

### Assembly Directives

Directives are instructions to the **assembler**, not the CPU:

| Directive | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `.MODEL` | Define memory model |
| `.STACK` | Reserve stack space |
| `.DATA` | Start data segment |
| `.CODE` | Start code segment |
| `PROC`/`ENDP` | Define procedure |
| `SEGMENT`/`ENDS` | Define segment |
| `EQU` | Define constant |
| `DB`/`DW`/`DD` | Define byte/word/dword |

### A Complete Program Structure

```asm
; ========================================
; Simple Assembly Program Template
; ========================================

.MODEL SMALL        ; Small memory model (64KB code + 64KB data)
.STACK 100h         ; 256-byte stack

.DATA               ; Data segment begins
    message DB 'Hello, World!$'

.CODE               ; Code segment begins
MAIN PROC
    ; Initialize data segment
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Display message
    MOV AH, 09h
    LEA DX, message
    INT 21h
    
    ; Exit to DOS
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** The `INT 21h` instruction invokes DOS services. Function 09h displays a string, and function 4Ch terminates the program.

sections.title: Memory Organization
sections.content:
## Memory in the 8086

### Physical Memory Organization

The 8086 has a 20-bit address bus, allowing it to address 1 MB of memory (addresses 00000h to FFFFFh).

### Segmented Memory Model

Because 8086 registers are only 16 bits, the processor uses **segmentation** to achieve 20-bit addressing:

```
Physical Address = (Segment Г— 16) + Offset
```

This is often written as:

```
Segment:Offset вҶ’ Physical Address
```

### Segment Types

| Segment | Register | Purpose |
|---------|----------|---------|
| Code | CS | Stores executable instructions |
| Data | DS | Stores program variables |
| Stack | SS | Stores stack data (PUSH/POP) |
| Extra | ES | Additional data segment |

### Calculating Physical Addresses

```asm
; Example: DS = 0400h, SI = 003Ch

; Physical address = (DS Г— 16) + SI
;                 = (0400h Г— 16) + 003Ch
;                 = 04000h + 003Ch
;                 = 0403Ch

; In assembly, memory access using [DS:SI]
MOV AL, [SI]    ; Loads byte at physical address 0403Ch
```

### Memory Organization Diagram

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                    1 MB Memory Space                      в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ FFFFFh в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ      BIOS ROM Area          в”Ӯ                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ F0000h в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ     Free Memory Area         в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ     (User Programs)          в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 04000h в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ    Program Data Segment      в”Ӯ вҶҗ Example: DS    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 04000h в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ                               в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ     Interrupt Vector Table   в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 00000h в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Byte Ordering (Endianness)

The 8086 uses **little-endian** byte ordering:

```asm
; To store the word 1234h at address 1000h:

; Address 1000h gets the LOW byte (34h)
; Address 1001h gets the HIGH byte (12h)

; Memory dump:
; 1000h: 34 12
```

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** The little-endian format means the least significant byte comes first in memory. This is important when debugging memory dumps or exchanging data with other systems.

sections.title: Register Overview
sections.content:
## 8086 Register Set

The 8086 has a rich set of 16-bit registers, with some split into 8-bit halves for compatibility with the 8080.

### General Purpose Registers

These registers can hold data and participate in arithmetic and logical operations, but each has historical specializations:

#### AX (Accumulator Register) - 16-bit
- Primary accumulator for arithmetic operations
- Used implicitly by MUL, DIV, CBW, CWD
- Divided into AH (bits 8-15) and AL (bits 0-7)

```asm
MOV AX, 1234h    ; AX = 1234h (AH=12h, AL=34h)
MOV AL, 0FFh     ; AX = 12FFh (AL modified)
MOV AH, 00h      ; AX = 0034h (AH modified)
```

#### BX (Base Register) - 16-bit
- Base pointer for memory addressing
- Divided into BH and BL
- Used as a pointer register (base address)

```asm
MOV BX, 1000h    ; BX holds a base address
MOV AX, [BX]     ; Load from address in BX
```

#### CX (Counter Register) - 16-bit
- Loop counter (used by LOOP instruction)
- Shift and rotate count
- Divided into CH and CL

```asm
MOV CX, 10       ; Set up counter for 10 iterations
loop_start:
    ; loop body
    LOOP loop_start   ; CX--, jump if CX != 0
```

#### DX (Data Register) - 16-bit
- I/O port addressing
- MUL/DIV extended results
- Divided into DH and DL

```asm
MOV DX, 0FFh    ; DX used as I/O port address
MOV AH, 09h
INT 21h         ; Display using port in DX
```

### Pointer and Index Registers

#### SP (Stack Pointer) - 16-bit
- Points to the current stack top
- Modified by PUSH, POP, CALL, RET
- Cannot be used directly in most operations

#### BP (Base Pointer) - 16-bit
- Access stack-based parameters
- More flexible than SP for memory access
- Preserved across calls

#### SI (Source Index) - 16-bit
- Source pointer for string operations
- Memory offset addressing
- Auto-incremented by string instructions

#### DI (Destination Index) - 16-bit
- Destination pointer for string operations
- Memory offset addressing
- Auto-incremented by string instructions

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** SI and DI are essential for string operations like MOVSB, CMPSB, and LODSB. The 8086 has dedicated hardware to process strings efficiently using these registers.

sections.title: The MOV Instruction
sections.content:
## MOV - Data Transfer Instruction

The MOV instruction is the most fundamental data transfer instruction. It copies data from a source to a destination.

### Syntax

```asm
MOV destination, source
```

### Valid Operands

| Destination | Source | Example |
|-------------|--------|---------|
| Register | Immediate | `MOV AX, 1234h` |
| Register | Register | `MOV AX, BX` |
| Register | Memory | `MOV AX, [1000h]` |
| Memory | Register | `MOV [1000h], AX` |
| Register | Segment reg | `MOV AX, DS` |
| Segment reg | Register | `MOV DS, AX` |
| Memory | Immediate | `MOV BYTE PTR [BX], 0` |

### Examples

```asm
; Immediate to register
MOV AX, 0          ; AX = 0
MOV CX, 0FFFFh     ; CX = 65535 (max unsigned word)

; Register to register
MOV AX, BX         ; Copy BX value to AX
MOV SI, DI         ; Copy DI value to SI

; Memory operands
MOV AX, [1000h]    ; Load AX from memory address 1000h
MOV [2000h], DX     ; Store DX to memory address 2000h

; With register indirect addressing
MOV BX, 1000h
MOV AX, [BX]       ; Load from address in BX
```

### Important Restrictions

1. **No memory-to-memory moves**
   ```asm
   ; ILLEGAL!
   MOV [1000h], [2000h]   ; Cannot!
   
   ; Must use register as intermediate
   MOV AX, [2000h]        ; Load to AX
   MOV [1000h], AX        ; Store from AX
   ```

2. **CS cannot be destination**
   ```asm
   ; ILLEGAL!
   MOV CS, AX             ; Cannot modify CS directly
   ```

3. **Immediate to segment register**
   ```asm
   ; ILLEGAL!
   MOV DS, 1000h          ; Cannot load segment directly
   
   ; Must use general register
   MOV AX, 1000h
   MOV DS, AX             ; Correct way
   ```

4. **Same size operands**
   ```asm
   ; ILLEGAL!
   MOV AX, BL             ; 16-bit to 8-bit
   
   ; Correct
   MOV AL, BL             ; 8-bit to 8-bit
   MOV AX, BX             ; 16-bit to 16-bit
   ```

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** MOV doesn't affect any flags. It simply copies bits from source to destination.

sections.title: Memory Addressing Modes
sections.content:
## Addressing Modes in 8086

The 8086 supports multiple ways to specify the location of memory operands.

### 1. Immediate Addressing

Data is embedded directly in the instruction:

```asm
MOV AX, 1234h      ; Data is part of instruction
MOV CX, 100        ; Decimal value
MOV AL, 'A'        ; ASCII code
```

### 2. Register Addressing

Data is in a register:

```asm
MOV AX, BX         ; Copy BX to AX
MOV SI, DI         ; Copy DI to SI
```

### 3. Direct Memory Addressing

A constant address is specified:

```asm
.DATA
    myVar DW 0
.CODE
    MOV AX, [myVar]        ; Load from label address
    MOV [1000h], AX        ; Absolute address
```

### 4. Register Indirect Addressing

Address is in a base or index register:

```asm
MOV BX, OFFSET myVar       ; BX holds address
MOV AX, [BX]               ; Load from address in BX
```

**Valid registers for indirect:**
- BX, BP (base registers)
- SI, DI (index registers)

### 5. Based Relative Addressing

Base register + constant offset:

```asm
MOV AX, [BX+4]             ; Address = BX + 4
MOV AX, [BP-2]             ; Address = BP - 2
MOV AX, [BX+100h]          ; Address = BX + 256
```

### 6. Indexed Relative Addressing

Index register + constant offset:

```asm
MOV AX, [SI+10h]           ; Address = SI + 16
MOV AX, [DI+1000h]         ; Address = DI + 4096
```

### 7. Base-Index Relative Addressing

Base + Index + optional offset:

```asm
MOV AX, [BX+SI]            ; Address = BX + SI
MOV AX, [BX+DI+4]          ; Address = BX + DI + 4
```

### Summary Table

| Mode | Example | Address Calculation |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| Direct | `[1000h]` | Constant |
| Register Indirect | `[BX]` | Register |
| Based | `[BX+4]` | Register + constant |
| Indexed | `[SI+10h]` | Index + constant |
| Base-Index | `[BX+SI]` | Base + Index |
| Based-Indexed | `[BX+DI+4]` | Base + Index + offset |

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** BP defaults to the Stack Segment (SS), while BX, SI, and DI default to the Data Segment (DS). This is why `[BP]` accesses stack data.

sections.title: Data Transfer Examples
sections.content:
## Practical Data Transfer Patterns

### Setting Up Data Segment

Every program must initialize DS before accessing data:

```asm
.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100h
.DATA
    value1 DW 1234h
    value2 DW 5678h
    result DW ?
.CODE
MAIN PROC
    ; Initialize data segment (required!)
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Now we can access our data
    MOV AX, value1      ; MOV AX, [value1] - load from memory
    MOV BX, value2
    ADD AX, BX          ; AX = 1234h + 5678h = 68ACh
    MOV result, AX      ; Store result
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

### Swapping Two Values

```asm
.DATA
    X DW 1234h
    Y DW 5678h
.CODE
    ; Swap X and Y using AX as temporary
    MOV AX, X          ; AX = 1234h
    XCHG AX, Y          ; AX = 5678h, Y = 1234h
    MOV X, AX           ; X = 5678h
```

### Loading an Address (LEA)

LEA (Load Effective Address) puts the address, not the value, into a register:

```asm
.DATA
    buffer DB 80 DUP(?)
.CODE
    LEA BX, buffer      ; BX = offset of buffer
    
    ; Compare to:
    MOV BX, OFFSET buffer  ; Same result!
    
    ; LEA is more flexible with displacement:
    LEA SI, buffer[DI]   ; SI = address of buffer + DI
```

### String Transfer

```asm
.DATA
    source DB 'Hello$'
    dest DB 80 DUP(?)
.CODE
    MOV SI, OFFSET source    ; Source address
    MOV DI, OFFSET dest      ; Destination address
    MOV CX, 5                ; Count = 5 bytes
    
next_char:
    MOV AL, [SI]             ; Load from source
    MOV [DI], AL             ; Store to destination
    INC SI
    INC DI
    LOOP next_char
```

> рҹ’Ў **Tip:** The XCHG instruction swaps two operands. It's useful for swapping values without a temporary register.
